title: To His Slave. - Translations From Horace.
content:
OD. i. 38.


    Persian grandeur I abhor;
    Linden-wreathed crowns, avaunt:
    Boy, I bid thee not explore
    Woods which latest roses haunt:

    Try on nought thy busy craft
    Save plain myrtle; so arrayed
    Thou shalt fetch, I drain, the draught
    Fitliest 'neath the scant vine-shade.
meaning:
Charles Stuart Calverley's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "To His Slave. - Translations From Horace."...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
